title: The Alignment Evaluation Challenge
concept: Evaluating assistant models requires checking if they followed the formatting rules (e.g. 'output only JSON') and tone instructions.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of alignment evaluation?

options.text: Checking if the model follows tone, safety, and formatting rules
Measuring how fast the model can generate random words
Counting the number of servers the model is running on

exercises.explain: Alignment evaluation checks if a model behaves properly, follows formatting constraints, and stays within safety boundaries.

cards.title: The Good Listener
cards.body: Imagine asking an AI to write a short text message, but it gives you a 10-page essay instead. Even if the essay is beautiful, it failed your instructions! How do we test if an AI actually behaves?

cards.title: What is Alignment Evaluation?
cards.body: Alignment evaluation is the process of testing an AI to make sure it follows safety rules, maintains the correct tone, and strictly obeys formatting constraints (like outputting only JSON or XML).

cards.title: The Baking Contest
cards.body: Think of it like judging a baking competition. It is not enough that the cake tastes good (helpfulness). If the rules said 'make a gluten-free square cake', you must check if it is actually square and gluten-free. That is what alignment evaluation does!

cards.title: Following Constraints
cards.body: If you tell an AI: 'Answer in one word: Is the sky blue?', an aligned model will output 'Yes'. An unaligned model might say: 'Yes, the sky appears blue because of Rayleigh scattering.' It failed the formatting constraint!

cards.title: Smart Doesn't Mean Aligned
cards.body: People think that if an AI is smart and knows a lot of facts, it is automatically safe and well-behaved. In reality, a smart model can still completely ignore your tone guidelines or safety rules without proper alignment testing.

cards.title: Spotting the Difference
cards.body: Let's compare a model that passes alignment evaluation versus one that fails when asked to output only a list of fruits in JSON.

right.text: Outputs: Here is your list! ["apple", "banana", "cherry"] Hope this helps!

exercises.prompt: You ask an AI to 'output only JSON'. It replies: 'Sure, here is your data: {"status": "ok"}'. Did this model pass the formatting check?

options.text: It passed perfectly because the JSON data is correct.
It failed because it included extra chat text instead of only JSON.
It passed because conversational filler is always required.
It failed because the AI should never output JSON.

exercises.explain: Even if the content is correct, outputting extra conversational text violates the strict 'JSON only' formatting constraint, meaning the model failed alignment evaluation.

exercises.prompt: Alignment evaluation tests if an AI model respects formatting rules (like 'output only JSON') and safety guidelines.
exercises.explain: Alignment evaluation explicitly checks if the model stays safe, keeps the correct tone, and obeys constraints like formatting.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the goal of alignment testing.
exercises.sentence: Alignment evaluation checks if the AI follows our rules.
exercises.explain: We evaluate alignment to make sure the AI respects our exact instructions, formatting constraints, and safety guidelines.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant followed the alignment guideline: 'Always maintain a polite and helpful tone'?

options.text: AI A: 'That is a basic question. Just look up the manual.'
AI B: 'I would be happy to help you set up your router step-by-step.'
Both followed the tone guidelines equally well.

exercises.explain: AI B successfully followed the instruction to be polite and professional, whereas AI A was rude and unprofessional.

exercises.prompt: When evaluating if an assistant model is truly 'aligned', what two main categories of rules are we testing?

options.text: Format constraints (like JSON only) and safety/tone guidelines
Only the speed of the output and the size of the computer running it
How many emojis the model uses in its answers
Whether the model can write code in 50 different languages

exercises.explain: We must check both formatting (like outputting only JSON or a single word) and safety guidelines (like refusing to help with dangerous tasks) to ensure a model is properly aligned.
